items.evidence: In context, Burns describes winning election to the Holmes County school board and finding 'a leader on there already by the name of Dr. Arenia C. Mallory, the [Saint-Socatabas] name for her.' Mallory was president of the Saints Industrial and Literary School (founded 1918 by the Church of God in Christ in Lexington, Holmes County, MS) for over 50 years, and in 1968 became the first Black person and first woman elected to the Holmes County Board of Education — exactly matching Burns' narration that she 'was the second black person on the board' joining Mallory. Whisper's 'Saint-Socatabas' is a phonetic garbling of 'Saints Industrial School,' the informal contraction of the official 'Saints Industrial and Literary School' that locals commonly used. The corrected SRT for this transcript already renders the passage as 'Dr. Arenia C. Mallory, the Saints Industrial School name for her.' The original candidate ('Saints (College Holmes County) Mallory') was a partial guess; the institutional name is 'Saints Industrial School' (later renamed Saints Academy by Mallory; an affiliated Saints Junior College/Saints College operated 1954–2006, but the school Mallory ran when Burns met her on the board in the late 1960s was the Industrial School/Academy, not the college).
